claim: Hand B exhibits the same Markov-1-flat character-level signature as Hand A — closest to random/Markov baselines on 4 or more of 6 character-level metrics. If confirmed, the generative-process hypothesis from H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 is manuscript-wide, not Hand-A-specific, and both hands were produced by similar (if not identical) generative mechanisms. If refuted (Hand B closer to natural language), the Hand A Markov-flat finding is hand-specific and the two hands may reflect different encoding regimes.
evidence_for: H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 CLOSEST_TO_RANDOM (4/6) found Hand A's character-level statistics closest to Markov-1 generation on 4 of 6 metrics (M1 entropy, M2 conditional entropy, M3 reachability, M4 Zipf). The M2 finding — H_cond(Hand A) = 2.19 vs H_cond(Markov_HA) = 2.20 — is near-identical by construction, confirming Hand A has no structure beyond first-order bigrams at the character level.

Currier (1976) established Hand A and Hand B as statistically distinct at the token-frequency level. But both share some overall manuscript-level properties (same scribes/period/substrate). Whether the Markov-flat signature holds for B as well is a direct empirical question.

If the generative process hypothesis is correct, both hands should show the signature — unless the two hands were produced by different generative mechanisms. Either outcome is informative: shared signature supports a uniform generative theory; divergence supports a two-system theory.

Methodology is identical to CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 (fetched corpora, fixed metric definitions, locked decision rule). The only change is the target corpus (Hand B tokens instead of Hand A tokens). Comparison baselines (Basque/Hungarian/Turkish natural, Esperanto constructed, shuffled Hand B + Markov-1 Hand B random) are recomputed for Hand B's character distribution.
evidence_against: Hand B has more tokens (~23,700) than Hand A (~11,000), giving more robust statistics but also potentially more structure simply from larger sample size. Higher-order regularities that are noise-suppressed in Hand A may become visible in Hand B, potentially shifting H_cond upward even if the underlying generative process is identical.

Hand B is known from prior Brain-V work to have longer mean word length (4.47 vs Hand A's 4.24, from H-BV-DENSITY-DIAGNOSIS-01 M2). Longer words could carry more character-level structure — making Hand B look less Markov-flat.

If Hand B shows natural-language H_cond (~3.5), the interpretation bifurcates: either the generative theory is wrong for the whole manuscript, or Hand A and Hand B represent different regimes (Hand A generative, Hand B natural). The latter is not impossible but requires explaining why a single 15th-c. manuscript would contain both types.

Alphabet size matters for the Markov baselines. Hand A and Hand B have overlapping but slightly different character inventories. The shuffled/Markov baselines for Hand B are regenerated from Hand B's own distribution — this preserves fairness per hand.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE (identical to H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 methodology, target changed to Hand B).

  CORPORA:
    Hand B: tokens from voynich-corpus.json filtered to currier_language=='B'. Concatenated with spaces, lowercased, whitespace-normalised.
    Reference corpora (identical to CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01):
      UD_Basque-BDT FORM column
      UD_Hungarian-Szeged FORM column
      UD_Turkish-IMST FORM column
      Esperanto Wikipedia sample plaintext
    Random baselines (regenerated for Hand B):
      Shuffled Hand B: random re-sampling with replacement from Hand B's character distribution (seed 42)
      Markov-1 Hand B: one-at-a-time sampling from Hand B's bigram distribution, length matched (seed 43)

  PREPROCESSING, METRICS, COMPARISON, AND DECISION RULE:
    IDENTICAL to H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 (M1 normalised Shannon entropy, M2 conditional entropy, M3 Markov reachability, M4 Zipf slope top-1000, M5 TTR at 5000-token window, M6 Heaps slope).
    For each metric, compute Hand B's value and measure distance to (natural_mean, constructed, random_mean).
    Classify closest category per metric; count across 6 metrics.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED_MARKOV_B: Hand B closest to random on >= 4 of 6 metrics. Same signature as Hand A. Manuscript-wide generative process.
    REFUTED_MARKOV_B: Hand B closest to natural on >= 4 of 6 metrics. Hand A-specific signature. Two-system interpretation.
    CLOSEST_TO_CONSTRUCTED_B: >= 4 closest to Esperanto. Unlikely given Hand A result but possible.
    PARTIAL_B: 3-3 split or no clear majority. Hand B has hybrid character.

  SHARP DIAGNOSTIC CHECK (LOCKED, independent of main decision rule):
    SPECIFIC M2 CHECK: compare H_cond(Hand B) to H_cond(Hand A) = 2.19 and natural range ~3.5.
      If H_cond(Hand B) in [2.0, 2.5]: MARKOV-FLAT-CONFIRMED (regardless of other metrics). Report separately.
      If H_cond(Hand B) >= 3.0: NATURAL-LIKE-M2 (regardless of other metrics). Report separately.
      If H_cond(Hand B) in [2.5, 3.0]: INTERMEDIATE.
    This check is informative even when the main 4/6 decision goes either way.

  REPORTING: same table format as CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 with Hand B as target. Include side-by-side comparison of Hand A and Hand B values for each metric.
test_metric: 6 metrics on Hand B, classified as closest-to-natural / constructed / random per metric. Count aggregated. Independent M2 sharp diagnostic.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED_MARKOV_B if 4+ random. REFUTED if 4+ natural. PARTIAL if 3-3. Separate MARKOV-FLAT-CONFIRMED on M2 if H_cond(B) in [2.0, 2.5].

tests_run.m2_band_check: Hand B H_cond = 2.02 falls in [2.0, 2.5] MARKOV-FLAT band decisively. Even more flat than Hand A (2.19). Natural-language band [3.0+] is far above.
tests_run.hand_a_vs_hand_b_summary: Hand A and Hand B produce IDENTICAL closest-baseline profile (natural: 2, constructed: 0, random: 4). Hand B's M2 H_cond (2.02) is slightly lower than Hand A's (2.19) — Hand B is even more Markov-flat at the character level. Both hands show the same generative signature. Hand B differs from Hand A primarily in Zipf slope (1.06 vs 0.97, steeper) and word-level metrics (slightly more repetitive), but both share the core Markov-1-flat character-level structure.

verdict_note: CONFIRMED_MARKOV_B (4/6) + M2 MARKOV_FLAT_CONFIRMED. Hand B replicates Hand A's Markov-1-flat character-level signature with striking fidelity — same 2-natural / 0-constructed / 4-random split, same metric-by-metric closest-category assignments. H_cond(Hand B) = 2.02, slightly MORE Markov-flat than Hand A's 2.19. Both hands' H_cond values are near-identical to their Markov-1 baselines (Hand A 2.19 vs Markov_HA 2.20; Hand B 2.02 vs Markov_B 2.01), confirming both hands have essentially no structure beyond first-order bigrams at the character level. The generative-process hypothesis is MANUSCRIPT-WIDE: both hands were produced by similar (though parameter-varying) generative mechanisms. Currier's A/B distinction remains valid at the word-frequency level (Hand B's Zipf 1.06 vs Hand A's 0.97; more repetitive TTR 0.34 vs 0.36) but the underlying generative signature is shared.
findings_for_followup: CRITICAL FOR v2: the generative-process hypothesis is MANUSCRIPT-WIDE, not Hand-A-specific. Both Hand A and Hand B exhibit Markov-1-flat character-level structure (H_cond 2.19 and 2.02 respectively, natural-language baseline ~3.5). The structural characterisation in v2 applies to the whole manuscript, not just Hand A.

HAND A VS HAND B DIFFERENCES PERSIST: Hand B has steeper Zipf slope (1.06 vs 0.97), slightly more repetitive TTR (0.34 vs 0.36), slower vocabulary growth (Heaps 0.86 vs 0.88). These fit the longer-word / more-structured-output interpretation of Hand B established in prior Brain-V work. The two hands are PARAMETER VARIANTS of the same underlying generative mechanism, not fundamentally different regimes.

Interpretation for v2: the Currier A/B split may be two instances of the same generator applied with different parameters — different 'table settings', different input material fed through the same encoding process, or consistent scribal practice applied twice. The generative mechanism is shared even where the output statistics differ.

CONSISTENCY CHECK ON THE BASQUE NARRATIVE: H-BV-AGGLUTINATIVE-EXPANSION-01 found Hand A matches Basque on 6/6 aggregate distributional metrics. The Markov-flat finding does not contradict this — aggregate distributions (morphemes/word, productivity, gap density) can emerge from generative processes whose outputs happen to share some structural features with natural languages. The two findings coexist: character-level generative signature + word-level Basque-compatible distributions. Hand B likely shows a similar relationship; worth running the Basque-distributional comparison on Hand B as a follow-up.

This test has implications for the Rugg-type grille / cipher-wheel hypothesis class. H-BV-RUGG-GRILLE-01 refuted Rugg's specific mechanical grille, but the class of generative processes that produce Markov-1-flat character-level statistics with word-level repetition patterns is now supported for BOTH hands. Candidate next-level test: what specific generative process, parameterised how, best reproduces each hand's statistical signature?

METHODOLOGY LESSON (additional): a structural finding in ONE hand is not manuscript-wide until tested. H-BV-M5-RIGIDITY-DIAGNOSIS-01 validated the M5 = 1.00 rigidity as genuine in Hand A. H-BV-HAND-B-MARKOV-01 now shows Hand B has the same generative signature, so the rigidity explanation (generative constraint at Markov-1 level) is likely shared. But this chain should be explicit in any paper — the structural characterisation of Hand A was established first, and the extension to Hand B is a separate verification.

NATURAL FOLLOWUP: test whether Hand B also matches Basque's morpheme-level aggregate distribution profile (analogous to AGGLUTINATIVE-EXPANSION-01). If yes, the Basque-compatibility is manuscript-wide; if no, the Basque aggregate-distribution match is Hand A-specific even as the Markov-flat character signature is shared.
reasoning: H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 found Hand A's character-level signature is Markov-1-flat (H_cond 2.19 vs natural 3.5). The most important follow-up is whether Hand B shares this signature. If yes, the generative-process hypothesis is manuscript-wide; if no, the finding is hand-specific and the two hands represent different regimes. The methodology is locked-by-reference to CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 (same metrics, same baselines, same decision rule framework) with the only substitution being the target corpus. An independent M2 check provides a sharp diagnostic that holds even if the 4/6 threshold isn't decisively met: H_cond(Hand B) near 2.2 is strong Markov-flat evidence; near 3.5 is strong natural-language evidence; between is intermediate.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed extension of H-BV-CONSTRUCTED-SIGNATURE-01 CLOSEST_TO_RANDOM verdict. Reference corpora (Basque UD, Hungarian UD, Turkish UD, Esperanto) already fetched 2026-04-17.
